question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger all three subquery branches (lower, inner, upper) simultaneously by crafting token.id and serialnumber range filters, causing getQuery() to emit a three-way UNION ALL query that multiplies DB work by 3x per request, exhausting DB CPU across 30%+ of mirror node replicas under moderate concurrent load?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shared mutable params array passed by reference into each getSubQuery() call to cause parameter index misalignment, producing malformed SQL that triggers repeated DB parse errors and error-handling overhead, degrading DB processing capacity across nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a large number of spender.id eq filters so that spenderIdInFilters grows unboundedly, causing getSubQuery() to build an IN clause with hundreds of parameters, generating a query that forces a full nft table scan and exhausts DB CPU on every node processing the request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests where lower, inner, and upper filter arrays are all non-empty, forcing getQuery() to construct a UNION ALL of three full LEFT JOIN subqueries each with their own ORDER BY and LIMIT, causing the DB to execute three independent sequential scans and a merge sort, consuming enough CPU to degrade 30%+ of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft requests that cause getSubQuery() to be called multiple times with the same shared params array, causing params to grow with each call and producing $N parameter placeholders with N far exceeding the expected count, resulting in DB query plan failures or excessive memory allocation that degrades node processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filters.push(...spenderIdFilters) mutates the filters array in-place inside getSubQuery(), causing spenderIdFilters entries to be appended to the lower/inner/upper filter arrays, so that when getQuery() iterates over all three subqueries the same spenderIdFilters are applied three times, tripling DB filter work and amplifying query cost?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getNft()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the getNft() endpoint with concurrent requests using valid but non-existent tokenId and serialNumber pairs, causing pool.queryQuietly() to execute SELECT * FROM nft queries that each perform index lookups, exhausting DB connection pool slots and degrading 30%+ of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the three-subquery UNION ALL path in getQuery() and combine it with a maximum spenderIdInFilters list, so that each of the three subqueries contains a large IN clause, causing the DB to evaluate three expensive IN-list scans merged with UNION ALL, consuming enough resources to degrade 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user cause getSubQuery() to produce a WHERE clause with a very large number of AND conditions by supplying many spenderIdFilters and spenderIdInFilters simultaneously, forcing the DB query planner to evaluate a complex predicate tree that degrades query throughput across 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getNfts()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting on getNfts() to send a sustained stream of UNION ALL queries, each triggering the three-subquery path, causing sustained high CPU utilization on the DB backend shared by 30%+ of mirror node processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/nftService.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query where lower and upper are both non-empty but inner is empty, causing getQuery() to produce a two-subquery UNION ALL with an outer ORDER BY and LIMIT applied after the union, forcing the DB to sort and merge two full result sets, amplifying memory and CPU usage enough to degrade 30%+ of nodes?
